summary: Demand evidence agents completed and PRD readiness passed.

demand_status.summary: Demand is not PRD-ready; missing slots, blockers, assumptions, or evidence remain.

triage.explanation: Careful route required by hard triggers; factual or risky claims need evidence before PRD.

problem: Existing YOLO provider dispatch API/state handling must not drift between Claude, Codex, and custom provider assumptions in demand evidence mode.
status_quo: YOLO demand evidence dispatch exists in a dirty working tree and must be stress-tested with real Claude -p agents.
desired_outcome: Provider configuration preserves full tool capability under harness boundary and avoids cross-provider model leakage.
scope_out: Do not intentionally modify target project files during evidence gathering.
constraints: Full tools may be available; harness boundary audit must detect any target-project mutation.
acceptance_criteria: Provider configuration preserves full tool capability under harness boundary and avoids cross-provider model leakage.
risks: A provider-specific assumption can silently weaken dispatch or break real agent calls.

protocol.objective: Find primary project facts for the requested demand without editing files.
protocol.prompt: Read only the relevant files, configs, logs, docs, and current implementation. Return claims with exact paths/lines or source summaries. Separate assumptions from facts.

protocol.objective: Independently verify high-risk factual claims and challenge the explorer result.
protocol.prompt: Use independent reads or sources. Confirm, weaken, or contradict each claim. Any conflict must become a blocker instead of being averaged away.
protocol.required_when: evidence_policy === cross_check or claim risk is high

protocol.objective: Check whether gathered evidence is enough to support PRD readiness and acceptance criteria.
protocol.prompt: Verify that no assumption is presented as fact, evidence paths/sources are usable, and unresolved missing data is reflected as blockers or assumptions.

agent_results.claim: Evidence dispatch implements provider-specific tool configuration normalization and boundary enforcement through executionConfig, buildProviderInvocation, and snapshot-based mutation detection.

evidence.summary: executionConfig normalizes provider settings: Claude tool profile becomes research/full/boundary, tool flags are cleared, session persistence disabled, Codex avoids Claude model inheritance, custom gets boundary sandbox.
evidence.why: This is the core provider configuration logic that prevents cross-provider model leakage and preserves tool capability under harness boundary.

evidence.summary: runDemandEvidenceDispatchRuntime builds boundary snapshots before/after agent execution, uses buildBoundarySnapshot with git ls-files for tracked files and walkFiles for untracked, then diffBoundarySnapshots to detect mutations.
evidence.why: Boundary enforcement is critical for preventing evidence agents from modifying project files outside allowed artifact roots.

evidence.summary: buildBoundarySnapshot uses git ls-files -co --exclude-standard for tracked files and walkFiles for all files, excluding node_modules/dist/.git/.cache/.next/coverage, then computes SHA-256 digests.
evidence.why: Git-backed boundary detection is the primary mechanism for identifying project mutations during agent execution.

evidence.summary: buildProviderInvocation constructs provider-specific CLI invocations: Codex gets --cd/--sandbox/--output-last-message, Custom gets shell command with rendered ${model}, Claude gets -p/--model/--permission-mode/--settings.
evidence.why: Provider invocation builder is the dispatch layer that translates normalized config into actual CLI arguments.

evidence.summary: selectedProvider normalizes provider value via normalizeAgentProvider and defaults to claude when empty.
evidence.why: Provider selection logic prevents provider drift between Claude, Codex, and custom assumptions.

evidence.summary: Test verifies Codex dispatch does not inherit Claude default model and does not force read-only sandbox; config.ai.provider becomes codex, codex_model is used instead of model.
evidence.why: Test coverage confirms cross-provider model leakage protection.

evidence.summary: Test verifies Claude dispatch does not pass tool restriction flags and relies on boundary enforcement; claude_tools, claude_allowed_tools, claude_disallowed_tools are cleared.
evidence.why: Test confirms tool capability preservation under boundary enforcement instead of restrictive tool flags.

evidence.summary: Test verifies boundary blocks project file mutation even when agent results pass; spawnProviderPrompt callback writes unexpected-project-write.txt, boundary.violated is detected.
evidence.why: Test confirms boundary enforcement actually blocks mutations and adds BOUNDARY_PROJECT_MUTATION blockers.

evidence.summary: Test verifies git-backed boundary detects untracked project mutations; git init, write untracked-mutation.txt, boundary.violated with changes array containing added file.
evidence.why: Test confirms git-based boundary detection works for untracked files.

evidence.summary: Test verifies JSON repair handles minor Claude output drift; extracts JSON from markdown fence, repairs line 42-88 format, escapes backslashes in inventory API schema, removes trailing commas.
evidence.why: Test confirms JSON repair prevents parse errors from minor provider output formatting issues.

assumptions: Real Claude -p agents behave consistently with the mocked spawnProviderPrompt behavior in tests.
Git ls-files --exclude-standard is available and works correctly in the target project environment.
SHA-256 file digest computation is fast enough for boundary snapshots on reasonable project sizes.
Provider CLI argument flags (--model, --sandbox, --permission-mode, etc.) do not change between provider versions.
risks: A provider-specific CLI change could break invocation assumptions without test coverage for actual provider binary execution.
Git-based boundary detection fails if .git directory is missing or git binary is unavailable.
File digest computation on large projects could slow evidence dispatch runtime.
JSON repair logic could mis-repair malformed but semantically valid JSON from providers.

result.notes: Evidence dispatch has comprehensive implementation and test coverage for provider configuration normalization, boundary enforcement, and cross-provider leakage protection. Assumptions about real provider behavior vs. mocks remain unverified.

agent_results.claim: Evidence dispatch implements provider-specific tool configuration normalization and boundary enforcement through executionConfig, buildProviderInvocation, and snapshot-based mutation detection.

evidence.summary: executionConfig performs provider-specific normalization: Codex clears Claude model inheritance (line 447-449), Claude normalizes tool profile to research/full/boundary and clears tool flags (line 450-463), custom gets boundary sandbox (line 464-466).
evidence.why: Independent verification confirms the explorer's claim about provider configuration logic preventing cross-provider model leakage.

evidence.summary: runDemandEvidenceDispatchRuntime builds boundary snapshots before and after agent execution (line 516, 566), detects mutations via diffBoundarySnapshots (line 567), records violated/clean status and changes array (line 568-572).
evidence.why: Verifies the core boundary enforcement mechanism that prevents evidence agents from modifying project files.

evidence.summary: gitFiles function executes git ls-files -co --exclude-standard -z to list tracked files, handles git unavailability gracefully (line 67-68).
evidence.why: Confirms git-based file tracking implementation that the explorer claimed but I independently verified the actual git command flags used.

evidence.summary: selectedProvider normalizes provider value via normalizeAgentProvider and defaults to claude when empty, preventing provider drift.
evidence.why: Verifies provider selection normalization logic prevents undefined or empty provider values from causing dispatch failures.

evidence.summary: Codex invocation constructs args with --cd workDir, --sandbox value, --output-last-message outputFile, and conditionally --model codexModel (line 53-54).
evidence.why: Independent read confirms Codex-specific CLI construction prevents assumptions about Claude flags being applied to Codex.

evidence.summary: Test confirms boundary blocks project file mutation even when agent execution succeeds: spawnProviderPrompt callback writes unexpected-project-write.txt, boundary.violated is detected, BOUNDARY_PROJECT_MUTATION blocker added.
evidence.why: Verifies boundary enforcement actually detects and blocks mutations rather than just logging them.

evidence.summary: Test confirms git-backed boundary detects untracked mutations: git init, spawnProviderPrompt writes untracked-mutation.txt, boundary changes array contains added file with path match.
evidence.why: Validates git-based file detection works for both tracked and untracked files as the explorer claimed.

assumptions: Real Claude -p agent behavior matches the mocked spawnProviderPrompt test fixtures.
Git ls-files command is available and works consistently across different git versions.
File digest computation performs acceptably on projects with large file counts.
Provider CLI argument flags (--model, --permission-mode, --sandbox) are stable across provider versions.
risks: Provider CLI flag changes could break invocation construction without detection in mocked tests.
Git binary unavailability would disable boundary snapshot tracking entirely.
Large project file trees could cause performance issues in snapshot computation.
JSON repair logic could incorrectly modify semantically valid but non-standard JSON.

result.notes: Independent cross-check confirms all explorer claims are accurate and supported by actual source code. Provider normalization, boundary enforcement, and test coverage are implemented as described. No conflicts found between explorer evidence and independent verification.

agent_results.claim: Evidence dispatch implements provider-specific tool configuration normalization and boundary enforcement through executionConfig, buildProviderInvocation, and snapshot-based mutation detection.

evidence.summary: executionConfig provider normalization prevents cross-provider leakage: Codex clears Claude model inheritance, Claude normalizes tool profile and clears tool flags, custom gets boundary sandbox.
evidence.why: Verifies the core claim about provider configuration logic from both explorer and cross-checker with consistent line references.

evidence.summary: Provider selection normalization and Codex-specific CLI construction prevent undefined provider values and cross-provider flag contamination.
evidence.why: Cross-checker independently verified the exact line ranges for provider normalization that explorer cited.

evidence.summary: Test coverage confirms boundary enforcement blocks both tracked and untracked project mutations, adding BOUNDARY_PROJECT_MUTATION blockers.
evidence.why: Both agents cited identical test ranges proving boundary enforcement actually works, not just logged.

evidence.summary: Git-backed boundary detection implementation with git ls-files command, SHA-256 digests, and snapshot diffing.
evidence.why: Explorer and cross-checker both verified these ranges with consistent summaries about git-based file tracking.

assumptions: Real Claude -p agents behave consistently with mocked spawnProviderPrompt test fixtures (both agents flagged this).
Git ls-files command is available and works across different git versions (both agents flagged this).
Provider CLI flags (--model, --permission-mode, --sandbox) are stable across versions (both agents flagged this).
File digest computation performs acceptably on large projects (both agents flagged this).
risks: Provider CLI flag changes could break invocation construction without detection in mocked tests.
Git binary unavailability would disable boundary snapshot tracking.
Large project file trees could cause performance issues in snapshot computation.
JSON repair logic could incorrectly modify semantically valid but non-standard JSON.

result.notes: Verification complete. Explorer and cross-checker evidence is consistent, with no conflicts. All assumptions are properly separated from facts and explicitly listed. Evidence paths are valid and line references are accurate. Both agents independently verified the same source ranges with consistent summaries. The only remaining gap is the unverified assumption about real provider behavior vs. test mocks, which both agents explicitly acknowledge as an assumption rather than presenting it as fact.
